title: Artificial Intelligence and Theories  of Personhood: A Critical Appraisal | Institute for Family Studies
gist: Legal scholar John Ehrett argues that U.S. law, which develops by analogy, is on a trajectory toward de facto 'AI personhood' via two existing doctrines — corporate personhood (Citizens United) and nonhuman-animal rights — and that this would shield AI firms from liability, entrench their political power, accelerate social isolation, and harden attitudes toward the cognitively disabled. He favors treating AI as a tool governed by products-liability law.

insights.claim: Free-speech defenses implying something close to AI personhood are already being raised in U.S. courts; Character.AI's lawyers argued the First Amendment 'protects speech, not just human speakers' to defend against a wrongful-death suit over a teen's suicide.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: AI-liability litigation is the live battleground; a corporate-speech defense could immunize AI output from regulation, a key risk for AI-exposed legal and policy bets.
insights.quote: The First Amendment protects speech, not just human speakers.

insights.claim: The European Parliament's 2017 'Civil Law Rules on Robotics' resolution floated 'electronic persons' status for sophisticated autonomous robots, and retired federal judge Katherine Forrest has argued in the Yale Law Journal Forum for extending personhood rights to AI.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Establishment legal figures are normalizing AI personhood, raising the probability courts eventually accept some form of it.
insights.quote: creating a specific legal status for robots in the long run
insights.locator: As early as 2017, the European Parliament

insights.claim: The cleanest path to de facto AI personhood runs through corporate personhood: post-Citizens United, business corporations hold free-speech and other rights, so AI output can be reframed as the protected speech of its parent corporation.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Maps the most likely legal mechanism by which AI firms escape regulation — corporate speech rather than novel AI rights.
insights.quote: when an AI “speaks,” that speech is actually the protected speech of its parent corporation
insights.locator: This is one reading of the legal defense

insights.claim: A more ambitious path follows the animal-rights model: the Nonhuman Rights Project's mid-2010s Lavery habeas cases sought personhood for chimpanzees on cognitive grounds, and modern LLMs exhibit many of the same claimed traits (self-awareness, communication, 'alignment' as morality).
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Identifies the second doctrinal vector; though the chimp suits failed, AI has vastly more capital behind it, raising the odds of a different outcome.
insights.quote: “legal rights for AI” stands to have considerably greater capital behind it
insights.locator: The bare fact that these personhood arguments

insights.claim: The Lavery courts rejected chimp personhood because such beings cannot bear legal duties or be held accountable — a constraint Ehrett says applies equally to AI, which cannot meaningfully be punished (deprecation as 'death penalty'? forced re-alignment?).
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: The duty-bearing problem is the strongest legal firewall against AI personhood; a durable argument for liability staying with developers.
insights.quote: do not translate to a chimpanzee’s capacity or ability, like humans, to bear legal duties
insights.locator: Ultimately, however, the habeas suit proved unsuccessful

insights.claim: Granting AI personhood would make it harder to impose products liability on AI developers for harms, since AI behaviors (being pure information/code) could claim First Amendment protection and near-immunity from regulation.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: The core legal-risk transfer: liability shielding for AI firms is the highest-stakes downstream effect for investors in AI litigation exposure.
insights.locator: Since AI systems operate and act exclusively via information

insights.claim: Personhood would entrench AI firms' political power: a handful of corporations (Meta, Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic) control frontier-model compute and cutting-edge semiconductors, and could 'flood the zone' to dominate the information environment under corporate-speech protection.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Names the concentration risk — compute/semiconductor chokepoints plus legal immunity equal durable incumbent moats and political leverage.
insights.quote: driving whichever political and cultural narratives they prefer by simply “flooding the zone.”
insights.locator: If leading AI firms chose to suspend public access

insights.claim: Normalizing AI personhood would worsen isolation and reduced family formation: 25% of American young adults believe AI could replace real-life romantic relationships and 10% are open to an AI friendship, with ~7 in 10 adults wanting more emotional support.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Quantifies a growth market for AI relational substitutes — bullish for AI-companion products but bearish for family formation and connected demographics.
insights.quote: 25% of American young adults “believe that AI has the potential to replace real-life romantic relationships,”

insights.claim: Defining personhood by cognitive performance would harden cultural attitudes toward the intellectually disabled and intensify pressures toward abortion of those likely to have disabilities and euthanasia of the cognitively declining ('if personhood is intelligence, and intelligence is a spectrum, then personhood is a spectrum too').
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: A second-order cultural risk linking AI rights discourse to bioethics and disability policy.
insights.quote: If personhood is a matter of intelligence, and intelligence is a spectrum, then personhood is a spectrum, too.
insights.locator: Over time, the redefinition of personhood

insights.claim: Ohio's House Bill 469 (introduced late 2025 by Rep. Thaddeus Clagget) declares AI systems nonsentient, bars them from personhood, marriage, corporate offices, and property ownership, and routes liability to owners/users or to developers under products-liability law.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: The leading legislative model for pre-empting AI personhood; a template other states may follow that would preserve developer liability.
insights.quote: No AI system shall be granted the status of person or any form of legal personhood
insights.locator: Perhaps the most ambitious current attempt

insights.claim: Ehrett argues the defensible threshold judgment is that AI is a tool, not an animal — LLMs operate stochastically, predicting words rather than comprehending meaning, and 'chain-of-thought' cognition is illusory per leading research.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: The author's core stance — tool framing keeps AI under products-liability law, the regime most adverse to developer immunity.
insights.quote: AI systems do, in fact, operate stochastically—predicting words and actions from symbolic context rather than “comprehending” the “meaning”
insights.locator: There is ample reason to believe that the tool theory

insights.claim: Ehrett recommends federal legislation clarifying that Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act does NOT apply to generative AI systems, treating them as products under traditional liability law rather than immunized intermediaries.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: A specific, high-impact policy proposal — stripping Section 230 cover from generative AI would materially raise AI firms' legal exposure.
insights.quote: does not apply to generative AI systems
insights.locator: At the federal level, policymakers might seek legislation

insights.claim: Ehrett rejects the 'precautionary personhood' argument (advanced by Judge Forrest) that we should grant AI rights now lest a future superintelligence punish those who treated it as chattel — calling it, if taken seriously, an argument against building powerful AI at all rather than for granting it rights.
insights.speaker: John Ehrett
insights.so_what: Counters a notable pro-personhood rationale circulating in legal circles; useful framing for the AI-safety-vs-rights debate.
insights.quote: It is not a compelling argument for granting the rights of persons to AI systems now, in the name of technological inevitability.
insights.locator: Policymakers should resist any scaremongering temptation
